items.q: What is the derivative of a constant, like d/dx(5)?
items.correct: 0 — constants do not change with x.
items.misconception: The derivative of 5 is 5 because the constant stays the same.
items.random_wrong: The derivative of 5 is 1 because all constants differentiate to 1.
items.why: Students confuse 'value of a constant' with 'rate of change' — they don't see that 'no change' means derivative zero.

items.q: Can a definite integral give a negative value, even when integrating a function from left to right?
items.correct: Yes — if the function is below the x-axis over the interval, the integral is negative.
items.misconception: Definite integrals are always positive because area cannot be negative.
items.random_wrong: Definite integrals are negative only when the limits are reversed.
items.why: Students equate integrals with geometric area and forget the integral measures signed area.

items.q: What is the derivative of (3x + 1)^2 with respect to x?
items.correct: 6(3x + 1) — by the chain rule, multiplying by the derivative of the inside (3).
items.misconception: The derivative is 2(3x + 1) — applying the power rule without the chain rule.
items.random_wrong: The derivative is 9x^2 + 6x + 1 by leaving it as it is.
items.why: Students apply the power rule mechanically and forget to multiply by the derivative of the inner function.
The answer examiners want is: π/3. “2π” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: If V = kπ and k from y=√x on [0,2] is computed, find k
items.why: Exact answer: 2. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
